event: Start by identifying the trigger that brought up difficult emotions or thoughts. A trigger can be anything — from a major event to something very small, or even just a fleeting thought.
thought: An automatic thought appears in response to an event and sets off a wave of emotions. These emotions can intensify and generate new negative thoughts, creating a vicious cycle. To keep it from growing, try to notice the automatic thought as early as possible and write it down.
body: Recall the moment the trigger appeared and pay attention to your body. What exactly did you feel physically? Which unpleasant sensations did you notice?
behavior: How did you behave after these emotions appeared? What did you do — or, on the contrary, what did you avoid doing?
response: When you notice an automatic thought, it’s important to look at it carefully. Such thoughts often contain cognitive distortions and logical errors. Your task is to find a rational, more realistic response and turn it into a supportive belief.
conclusion: Describe the conclusions you came to: did your emotional state change, how do you now see this situation, and what would you like to try next time in similar circumstances?
comparison: Write down the arguments that support the automatic thought and those that contradict it. This will help you evaluate it more objectively and see the situation from a broader perspective.

event.title: Event
event.text: I made a mistake on a task at work

thought.title: Automatic Thought
thought.text: I feel like I make mistakes often, and because of that people around me treat me badly

body.title: Body sensations
body.text: I felt my heart start racing and a heaviness in my chest

behavior.title: Behavior
behavior.text: I abruptly shut the door and walked out

response.title: Alternative Response
response.text: Everyone makes mistakes — they’re a natural part of work and learning. One error doesn’t define me as a professional or as a person. I can’t know for sure what others actually think, and my assumptions about their attitude may be much harsher than reality.

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.text: If a similar situation happens again, I’ll try not to jump to conclusions about other people’s reactions. If I’m unsure, I can clarify the situation directly instead of guessing. I’ll also create a short checklist to review tasks before submitting them, so I can feel more confident and reduce the likelihood of similar mistakes.
